Alright guys, so today we are going to be training on the commitments. You guys obviously know what those all are. First and foremost, we're going to start off with the commitment of time. And what I'll do is I'm going to have each one of you read off the script. You guys will have your script with you. Bill, why don't you grab yours. It's all good. Now in the script they asked us to switch that from 5 to 10 to 10 to 15. Is that where you still want this to say though on the phone? The biggest thing I think with the time is that what you're saying, and let's say you're setting that to a closer, that you guys are lining up, because let's say you have a set of you say 5 to 10 hours a week, then the closer comes along and he says, okay it's 10 to 15. Well so and so Matt told me such and such. So you just want to make sure we're consistent with everything that we're telling these people. So what I'll do is I'll read that intro obviously right after the goals. We move into the commitments. To me the commitments, every part of this is very important, especially the commitments. They need to be in place here. The biggest thing obviously we start off with is time. But what I'll do is I'll read this and we'll go around the table and Cateval, I'll have you read the first one there. But it starts off there are four qualities we must have before I can make any recommendations for success teams. The first quality we must have is the commitment of time obviously right. So Cate, as far as the time commitment, what objections have you been hearing as far as time with people? A lot of people you know, if they're looking, usually it's not a problem, it's not really difficult to overcome that one. If they're unemployed obviously, but if you find somebody who's doing other ventures, has another job, has kids, things like that, then you will run into, well I don't know if I have time for that, I'm focusing on a promotion, I've been reading, you know, I've been doing other study courses for online training, and I just don't know if I can come up with that sort of time right now. But you really don't run into it too often. I haven't really been held up at all on that, but a couple of times I suppose. Absolutely, and these people all ordered this for a reason, and with time, you know, all these objections are always going to come up. I mean time and money, all these, the biggest two objections that people have, but with time, the way I overcome that, and if you guys want to write these down, is number one, is it's a lack of direction, not a lack of time. These people, they don't know what they're doing, that's why they order our information. They lack the direction, they don't know which way to go. A lot of times what I'll tell people is, you know, I talk to people all over the world, and people start off on step one or two, then they'll jump to step nine, back to five. So they really lack the structure, they lack the direction, not the time. I mean I always pull out the line, hey we all have 168 hours in a week, you can't make 10 hours, five to 10 hours a week to do this, or 10 to 15 hours rather. That's what I use, is 10 to 15 hours. I also tell them that time is the most important thing. I bring up that in any brick and mortar business, and a lot of these people, either run a business, have their own business, any brick and mortar business, it's going to take time, and I'll use the example, I don't care if it's this business, a restaurant down the street, a hot dog stand, takes time to make money. So that's the first one, you are, you know, you're going to get people all. I got family, and I got Christmas coming, and I got New Year's, then I got Valentine's, and I got little Johnny's birthday, those things are always going to happen in life. So, you know, the way you overcome that is, you know, you've been thinking about this for so long now, you know, what's really holding you back? And as you dig deeper a lot of times, it's because it'll have the money, but that's pretty self-explanatory, but right off the bat, when I tell people, I always let them know time is the most important thing, and they're thinking in the back of their mind, well, no, money is the most important thing. So, it's just kind of a little psychology I use with them. Carl, if you want to read the next part of the script there, the next quality. Just a little too? Yeah. We're looking for someone who's open-minded and teachable, so we can form a long-term mutual beneficial relationship, or you're comfortable and willing to learn from our experts. Okay. And it also says, do you feel comfortable in a student-teacher relationship? So, number one is time, number two, open-minded and teachable. Something that I do with open-minded and teachable guys is obviously I use the analogy of it's like going to school, it's a good one, and the reason I use that is because obviously, in some sort of a sense, these people are going back to school, and the last thing we want these people to do is come aboard, not put in the time and effort, and you'd be surprised. But really, with being open-minded and teachable, a question that I pose to them is, have you ever tried to teach a no at all? Obviously, throw that one out there to them. The reason I ask that question is it makes it real for them, they understand the process. Really, but that's pretty straightforward. You're not going to get a lot of resistance with this. If you do, you probably wouldn't have gotten this far in the script anyway. So, the thing that I like to ask under this, which is not in the script, is why are you open-minded and teachable? The questions, question based selling, why? Have them tell you and sell themselves. Always follow it up with a, okay, that's great. Just don't take a yes, dig into it a little bit. I like to dig into it a little bit. Let's see, Bill, do you want to, obviously, we'll go into money. I don't think you have to cover all of that. The last thing that we want is somebody procrastinating. You got to tackle them before you get them over to a closer. You can't breeze through them or you're going to be up to the creek without a paddle. So how long thinking about... Well, it's in between three and ten thousand. You just shot yourself in the foot, dead deal. Save the buy to that person. Because it's all about the psychology and you got to warm them up to what we're doing. But with the bracketing, on your script, how is the bracketing, boys and girls? I'll have you actually read that, Matt. It's on the next page. The gray? Yeah, there you go. Great. I do want to get an idea of what you would feel comfortable investing into your business to get it started. Some of our students invest ten to fifteen thousand, some even more, but most of our students invest between five to ten thousand to get their business started. Where would you see yourself investing to get started today? The top, middle or bottom. I posture a little bit differently and I'll share how I do that. Depending on the person, let's say I have somebody who has, I just probed somebody, they have fifty thousand dollars' debt and they have fifty thousand available. So they have a hundred thousand in total all together. Now with this person, I'm not going to shoot low like that. Because this, it could be a possibility to be a big deal depending on where they're at in their business. Now don't think just because they have fifty thousand available, they're a fifteen thousand dollar deal. That's not the case. But you want to posture it four year closer in the manner of, okay, well, Mr. Client Bob here, what I want you to understand is some of our students are very aggressive. They'll come in, they'll invest twenty to thirty grand with us. The reason they'll do that is because they already have an existing business, they're already making ten, twenty grand a month with their business. So that's very aggressive, I don't recommend it to anybody unless they're making good money already. The more moderate, they'll come in, they'll invest in between ten and twenty grand. Now those people, they have some things established, they might be making a couple grand a month, but they need more advanced techniques. Now the more conservative, they'll come in, they'll invest five to ten. And the reason I do that is because they have a lot more money. Now if I have somebody, let's say that's twenty grand in debt and they have seven K available, now I'm going to tone that down much, much lower. I might even go lower than the script. What I would do there is I'd be more of a thing, okay, the more aggressive they'll come in, they'll invest ten to fifteen, more moderate they'll come in and invest five to ten. And the most conservative will come in and invest anywhere from two to five. So you got to be a chameleon, depending on the case per case basis. I like to switch it up because we do have people that come in and invest up to thirty grand with us. I mean that's happened in the past, so you're not lying to the clients, but the biggest thing is to make sure your posture in the right manner, not closing any doors. They might only be, I mean this guy might only be a five grand deal, and that's up to your closer to the side. But bracketing guys, very important to what we do because what happens if they get on the phone with us as a closer and they don't have any idea and we throw out a number of eighty five hundred dollars and they don't know what this is going to cost them, they're going to hang up the phone on the closer. Now if you guys have already warmed them up, we did the five to ten minute call back and they get on the phone with us and we call them back and they pick up, the chances of that being a dealer even greater. So these are all very, very important. Do you guys have any resistance within any of these? Probably be the money the biggest to any of you guys. I just, you know, when you're trying to find what the balance is. Caves going to do it his way, Carl you're going to do it your way, same with you Bill, same with you Matt. You're going to find something that works for you. This works for a reason. You know, however you want to go about it, the psychology that's fine with the money and I'll cover that with you real quick. How I go about getting into the money is typically, you know, I'll start probing and I'm asking them about what they do and I'll get into the money and I'll be like, well, you know, everyone I talk to and I talk to people all over the world. And a lot of people I talk to, the biggest thing that they're wanting to do is get out of debt. Are you in the same position? Oh yeah, absolutely. I mean, nine out of ten people are in debt. So, they're going to say absolutely. Okay, well, is that a goal of yours? Absolutely, it's a goal of mine. Okay, so if Russell had to write you a check, I like to use that one, how much of that checkup did you need for it? Well, why are you asking about my credit? Well, the reason is, is obviously it takes money to make money. I don't know how much it's going to be to do this, but I want to be able to best help you. Okay, now I don't want to give you something that you don't need and at the same time, I don't want to undercut you either. I don't want to make sure we're providing you the things that you need to be successful. And a lot of times that will overcome it. The biggest thing, if someone's not wanting to give you their credit card information, you know, like I was telling you guys, how are we going to get the credit card number at the end of the deal? So I think a lot of it is when you're asking about it, also digging and asking about their interest rates. And the reason it's not relevant for us, we don't care about their interest rates, because we'll show them how to do a balance transfer. And that gets them 0%. And that's something more in the closing of the things. But the reason I ask is it's showing that I care about that debt, how much interest they're paying on that debt. And they're more than willing to give you that. And once you've gotten that far, okay, if you put all those cards in a pile, what kind of limits would they give you? That's the way I approach it. I know they like to get specific information. Absolutely. If you can get specific information about credit, that's always the best thing to do. Card per card per card. I mean, you have better chances of going to a closer and a closer looking. When we go to ask for the sale, we know exactly where to go. And okay, well, you have a 10,000 on Discover. Why don't we go ahead and put that on there? But let's also say on the credit cards, you're digging through and somebody says, yeah, I have a Visa and a Mastercard and an Amex. And I pay them all off monthly with that. And I have 5% interest on that. And my Visa has a $15,000 limit. My Mastercard has a $20,000 limit. I'm not going to dig any deeper. That's more than enough money for a deal. I'm going to say, great. You get me a good idea with where you're at. Now, if somebody like this guy who says, yeah, I got four or five cards and I got 20,000, that's probably about seven available. I'm going to dig with him because the last thing I want to do is throw over a set to somebody that the guy just lied to me about the money. And these people are going to lie to you about their money because they want to go to the closer. They want the information we have to provide for them. So just be careful. It's a case-by-case basis on who you're dealing with there. The biggest thing is we need a yes to all of these. Don't move forward to any of these until you've gotten a yes. And if they're giving you a yes too quickly, always dig deeper. Why? If you give over time and someone says, yeah, I got five to 10 minutes and then, or I'm not five to 10 minutes, sorry, 10 to 15 hours a week. That's not a problem. I can do that. No problem. Okay, well, great. I just want to make sure because this is a real business and I don't want to just breeze past that. I want to stop, take the time to explain it. Same thing with open-minded, teachable, all of these guys. You want to make sure that you're getting the yeses. And this should be the same all the way throughout your script. The way I set guys is I like to dig. It's question-based selling. If you guys want to write these down and we might have had them in our other training. If you notice in my cubicle, I have why, how come, and what else. Those are all good things, guys. As salesmen, we should be listening 70% of the time and talking 30% of the time. As you start to learn more about the business, you're going to have the tendency to want to talk. It happens. I do it all the time and I've got to catch myself. If you let these people talk, they're going to give you the information you need. Listen to Ravi on his sets. How much does he talk? It'll shoot you in the foot, guys, if you're doing it that way. Back to the yes. We need a yes on all of these. If somebody can't commit to a yes to all of these, are they a deal? Probably not. Can we turn them into a deal? Absolutely. If you have somebody balking at one of these and you get a wishy-washy answer and you're pushing, make sure you write that down for your closer so they know what objection to tackle with those people before we even pitch them. The whole reason is, at the end, when we're pitching these people, because when a closer gets on the phone, that's what we cover. We cover time, open mind and teachable, decision making, and money. Right after you guys did it, we'll get back on the phone 5-10 minutes later, get to know them, and whatever these things over again. The reason is, tie them down. Because at the end of the conversation, everything might make sense, and if we don't do these properly, someone's going to say, well, I really don't have the time. Well, hold on. You just told Cave that you did have the time, and at the beginning of our conversation, you told me. So we can use that power against them to tie them down and say, hey, I wouldn't have gone through this if you would have been up front and honest with me. And it'll happen from time to time, but as long as we're doing it this way, our odds are going to increase. But not only in the commitments am I looking for yes, all the way through the set, guys. The more times I can get somebody to say yes, not only in the set, but in the close. If what I'm going over with them, for example, let's say I'm talking about OPM, I'm going to say, okay, now using OPM, does that make sense to you? Of course it makes sense. Yeah, I'd love to use it makes money. Does that interest you? Yeah, absolutely it interests me. So if you guys want to write that down, it's a good one to follow up. Does that make sense? Does that interest you? I always use it in my clothes, and you can use that on a lot of different parts in the set, but the biggest point is getting these people to say yes, because if I'm getting them to say yes all the way through my set, then I go over and set a closer, and that closer is going through and getting them to say yes. At the end, when we say does that make sense? Does everything, does it all interest you? Absolutely. Okay, where am I shipping this to? Bam, we just got a deal. So the way I look at it, guys, it's kind of like a submarine. I'm not very good at drawing, but I'll try here. So you have your submarine, and in a submarine, you're underwater, you have different compartments. And what I'll do with people, so let's say this is the intro. Once I've gone through the intro, lack of knowledge right here, I'll close that off. They're tied down on that. They have a lack of knowledge. I'll come in here, I'll find the pain and goals and the money. Okay, I'll seal that off. They're all tied down on that. So that compartment's closed, so if any water comes in here, there's no way they can back out. Then I'll get down here finally to the commitments. So once time is done and I've tied them down on that, that's closed off. Once open mind and teachable, you've overcome that, you've explained it, you've gotten a yes. That's closed off and tied down. Same thing with decision making and money. I think you guys get the drift. So when the sidestep and when not to sidestep, guys, the commitment is not the place to sidestep. Always with the money, you want to make sure with the money here that you're not shooting yourself in the foot in the beginning of the probe. No matter how much or how persistent somebody is with you, make sure you are not throwing a number out there until the time is right and when they're ready for it. Maybe they're going to balk at the money when you've gotten that far, but at least they have a little better idea of what they're going to be doing and what the commitments are. Yeah, he actually does. And you want to sidestep it, but guys, by all means, I don't want to hear anybody talking about figures unless the person's broke. You know, I mean, anybody who has half a brain, they know these things are all necessary. But this is what's required of them. And like we talked about the other day, it's very important that they know what is required of them. It gives them that sense of, okay, I've got a role in all of this. Guys, also, you're going to encounter, let's talk about decision making real quick again. I know I'm jumping around, but with decision making, what kind of things do you guys hear in about getting started today and decision making? Have you guys ever gotten that far in your script? Speak it to the wife or husband, you know. Good point, okay? Absolutely. So with decision making, spouse, okay? And you guys have probably already caught on. Okay, we're here to create success stories, not divorce stories, right? So people are going to respect you for that, okay? The way we want it to work is either you're the final decision maker or we get both of you on the phone. The reason we want both of you on the phone is, let me ask you, Bob, how long have you been married for? Oh, I've been married 30 years. I would imagine after 30 years, you know your wife pretty well. Oh, yeah, yeah, yeah, yeah, yeah, I know my wife, excellent. So that's huge, guys. Don't ever forget about the spouse. I know we covered in the probe and thanks for bringing that up, Cave. But at the decision making, always bring up the spouse too or business partner. Now, if they're single, don't push it, okay? But tie them down on it again because you're going to have people, oh, no, I make all my own business decisions. That's one thing that bracketing does, okay? When you bracket somebody, that gives them idea if they can make that investment on their own or if they really do need to discuss it with their wife. Well, I always discuss things with my wife. Well, let me get her on the phone. Well, why don't you have your director go over with me and then I'll go back to my wife? Right. No, no, no, no, no. We don't want that to happen, okay? But with the posture, so what I'll do is, I guess I'll go through it. Let's say, okay, I've gotten through all time money. Everything's good. It's a go. I bracketed them. They're bracketed. The guy's bracketed at five to ten grand. And what I'll do is obviously on the script, you know, it says, what I need to do from here is put your money. What I need to do from here is put you on hold for a few minutes and see if I can grab the program director, spend some time with you. It says, grab a pen and a piece of paper and I'll be right back with you. So obviously you put them on hold. Don't be real urgent to get back on the phone with them. The longer you make them hold for, the better off, guys. Because you want to create the impression that you're walking over to the elevator. You're going up to the elevator to the top floor. You're going to talk to your director secretary. His secretary walks into the big wooden doors. You know, you want to create that impression that, hey, you're not going to the next cubicle down the aisle. You want to make sure that you're going to talk to the man. Okay, I'm going to put you on hold real quick. What I'm going to do is I'm actually going to go see if Russell's right-handed man has some time to speak with you. I know he's a real busy guy, but let me put you on hold real quick and see what I can do. Or what I like to say is, you know, I'm going to go speak to his secretary. That makes their closer seem even busier. You know what I mean? The last thing they want is some guy sitting around twirling his thumb just chomping at the bit to get a deal. You know, that's not very professional at all. So when you get back on the phone with them, hey, I got some good news and bad news. What do you want first? That's what I'll tell them. Okay, well, I want the bad news. They always want the bad news first. So, well, bad news is, he's actually tied up in a conference call. I do it every time. And he's not getting bad news. Yeah, well, bad news, yeah. He's tied up right now. He's busy. I'm probably not busy and probably getting a drink of water or talking to somebody. Playing the role. Yeah, exactly. So what you want to do is you want to make sure that that person understands, hey, this guy's busy. It's a privilege to talk to him. Now, he said, actually, his secretary said he's going to have some time to call you back in about 10, 15 minutes. Is that going to work for you? Oh, yeah, absolutely. Haven't called you back. Okay, great. Get off the phone. No. What you want to do there is you want to make sure that that person has 45 minutes to an hour to speak to the closer. The reason being, they get on the phone with the closer. The closer is 20 minutes into it and they got to run. It's just all about tapping them into that box and tying them down on these commitments. So before you get off the phone with that person for that appointment, make sure you tie them down, that they have 45 minutes to an hour to speak to the director. So it's very important that you go about that process. So it also talks about, sorry. Which is more important, if the, okay, I can give you about 20 minutes to right now, or we can make an appointment next Tuesday for 45 minutes. You still want them now, don't you? The problem is you're going to get a halfway through it and a closer is not going to give them a number in that amount of time. I wouldn't because I'm only going to give them a number if I know they have enough time to stay on the phone with me. I'm not going to throw a number out there precisely and say, okay, bam, they get off the phone after 20 minutes, then a week goes by and all they thought about was five, 10 grand. And because when we close, we give them a number, we don't pitch them the business first. We give them a number first, nine times out of 10 before we even spend our time. We don't want to waste our time either. You guys do give them a number pretty close to the front? Yeah, that's just part of closing stuff, guys. We're not going to spend our time. You guys don't want to talk about the money. Side step it as much as possible until you get down to the commitments, until you break it down to them. Tell them, of course there's going to be investment. Of course there's money involved. I don't know how much it's going to be. Let me ask you this. If it was free, would you do it? Of course I would. Well, what if we showed you how to do this without taking any money out of your own pocket? Well, absolutely I would do it. So, you know, as you come along, it's going to be second nature. Second nature. You're just riding a bike. They're going to throw these objections out to you. The biggest ones you're always going to get is no time and no money.
